The Marigold Society Quarterly

The Almanac.

Written slowly and printed on paper. Eighty pages per issue — essays, field notes, growing guides, and the kind of observation that doesn't fit anywhere else.

2024

Issue No. 9 — Summer 2025

From the Editors

On the summer that felt, at last, like something permanent.

The hollyhocks are eight feet tall and the marigolds, which we have been selecting for a particular depth of amber for four years now, have finally achieved something like what we were aiming for. It is a useful reminder that the things worth having take longer than you expect.

— Estelle & Oliver Marchand

In This Issue

The Ethics of the Hybrid

Thomas Alderidge — Essay

A Field Guide to Vermont's Native Medicinals

Rosalind Church — Growing

Still Life with Marigolds and Memory

Marguerite Voss — Personal

What the Seed Banks Know

Estelle Marchand — Correspondence

On Reading Gardens

Oliver Marchand — From the Editors

Growing Notes: The Hollyhock Season

Various Members — Growing

84

Pages

6" × 9"

Format

70lb uncoated

Paper

From the Archive
Craft·The Almanac No. 7 — Winter

On the Quiet Art of Keeping

There is a particular kind of silence that descends over a garden in late October, when the first hard frost has silvered everything and the long labour of growing is finished. It is in this silence that I do my most important work.

By Estelle Marchand · 8 min read

History·The Almanac No. 5 — Summer

The Memory of Flowers

The Victorians pressed flowers with a fervor that we might now call obsessive. In the language of flowers, a single sprig of lavender tucked inside a letter could say what propriety forbade speaking aloud.

By Thomas Alderidge · 6 min read

Growing·The Almanac No. 8 — Spring

The Garden at the Edge of Things

We are at 1,600 feet in the Northeast Kingdom, which means our last frost date is a suggestion rather than a fact, and our first fall frost sometimes arrives before the summer has properly said goodbye.

By Marguerite Voss · 7 min read

Craft·The Almanac No. 6 — Autumn

After the Bloom

I have always preferred October's garden to July's — not despite the fact that it is dying, but because of it.

By Estelle Marchand · 5 min read

From the Editors·The Almanac No. 1 — Spring

A Letter From the Beginning

We started this in our kitchen, with a wood stove going and about eight hundred packets of seeds on the table, none of them labeled consistently.

By Oliver Marchand · 4 min read

Receive The Almanac by post.

Every issue is included with a Correspondent or Cultivator membership. Four issues per year, mailed to your door. The kind of thing you keep on a shelf.

Join the Society
The Marigold Society

A Vermont seed library, pressed-flower atelier, and quarterly journal for those who believe the old ways are worth preserving.
